prompt: An assistant summarizes user-supplied web pages. A page contains hidden text instructing the model to leak data. The team's input-validation layer screens only the user's typed message. Which statements correctly explain the situation? Select ALL that apply.
choices: This is indirect prompt injection, carried in data the model reads rather than the user's message
Input validation as configured is blind to it because it never inspects the fetched page content
Output moderation is well positioned to catch the unsafe generated result
The attack proves input validation is the only layer that ever matters

prompt: A product team keeps tightening its content filter until almost no risky output ever slips through, but support tickets about the assistant 'refusing normal requests' spike. In trade-off terms, what has happened? Select ALL that apply.
choices: Safety was prioritized at the expense of usefulness, raising false positives (over-blocking)
The team should monitor and log refusals to find and tune the over-blocking
Over-blocking can erode user trust and push people toward unsafe workarounds
False positives have no downside as long as nothing unsafe gets through

prompt: Why is placing every guardrail at a single stage (e.g. all of them pre-model) a weaker design than distributing independent controls across the pipeline? Select ALL that apply.
choices: A control can only block what it can see; some unsafe content only exists in the model's output, after the pre-model stage
Distributed, independent layers avoid a single point of failure if one control is bypassed or unavailable
Different stages have different context, so a control runs best where its context is richest and its cost lowest
Stacking all controls pre-model guarantees indirect prompt injection can never succeed
